experiment: DISC_CANCER_4_v1 -- NSCLC External Validation: Does delta-r coupling sensitivity predict DepMap CRISPR essentiality?
description: Fourth iteration of the NSCLC discovery loop. The first three iterations produced a complete 16-node delta-r ranking: ERK1=-0.135, EGFR=-0.096, TP53=-0.090, PTEN=-0.080, HER2=-0.071, MEK1=-0.011, mTOR=+0.049, KRAS=+0.061, MDM2=+0.100. This experiment tests whether that ranking has external predictive validity against DepMap 23Q4 CRISPR essentiality scores -- the same source cited in the nsclc_egfr_signaling.json metadata (A549, NCI-H1299, HCC827, NCI-H460 cell lines). Three structural predictions are tested: (1) ERK1 is more essential than MEK1 in NSCLC lines. Rationale: delta-r|ERK1=-0.135 vs delta-r|MEK1=-0.011 -- ERK1 is 12x more structurally disruptive when removed. If coupling sensitivity predicts functional essentiality, ERK1 CRISPR score should be more negative (more essential) than MEK1. This directly tests whether the drug landscape is missing the right target: MEK inhibitors (trametinib) are approved, but ERK1 is the actual structural bottleneck. (2) TP53 is non-essential in NSCLC despite delta-r=-0.090 (third-most disruptive). Rationale: TP53 is loss-of-function mutated in ~50% NSCLC; established NSCLC cell lines (A549=R273H-mut, H1299=null, H460=wt, HCC827=wt-like) have variable TP53 status. Across the 4-line panel, mean CRISPR essentiality should be near-zero or non-essential -- not because TP53 is structurally unimportant, but because cancer cells have already removed it. This tests the 'structural void' hypothesis: delta-r detects LOSS of coupling structure, not a druggable active target. (3) PTEN is non-essential despite delta-r=-0.080 (fourth-most disruptive). Rationale: PTEN is loss-of-function in ~10% NSCLC; it is a negative regulator that dephosphorylates PIP3. Its high delta-r reflects its structural coupling role -- removing it disconnects the PI3K constraint on AKT1 -- but in cancer cell lines that already operate without PTEN suppression, CRISPR knockout has minimal survival effect. Dataset to run: examples/nsclc_with_depmap.json -- to be built after pre-registration commitment by downloading DepMap 23Q4 CRISPR data for the 16 target proteins in the 4 NSCLC lines and adding depmap_crispr_nsclc_mean as a node property. Pre-registration protocol: hypotheses committed, hash generated, pushed to public repo BEFORE downloading DepMap data. Framing note: delta-r is representational coupling instability (cross-layer synchronization sensitivity), not biological causality. This experiment tests whether the metric has external predictive validity, not whether it proves mechanistic causation.

hypotheses.statement: ERK1 depmap_crispr_nsclc_mean < MEK1 depmap_crispr_nsclc_mean -- meaning ERK1 is more essential (more negative score) than MEK1 across the 4 NSCLC lines (A549, NCI-H1299, HCC827, NCI-H460). Structural basis: delta-r|ERK1=-0.135 vs delta-r|MEK1=-0.011. ERK1 is the terminal MAPK effector that directly phosphorylates MYC (S62 stabilization) -- removing ERK1 breaks the KRAS-coexpression anchor that no other node replicates. MEK1 is ERK1's direct input, but MEK2 provides redundancy in cell survival that ERK1 does not have at the terminal node. If CONFIRMED (ERK1 score < MEK1 score): coupling sensitivity predicts functional essentiality; the structural analysis identifies ERK1 as a better target than MEK1, consistent with the clinical gap (no approved ERK inhibitor vs. approved MEK inhibitors). If DENIED (MEK1 more essential or equal): delta-r does not distinguish functional essentiality within the MAPK cascade; structural coupling and cell survival essentiality decouple at this level.

TP53 depmap_crispr_nsclc_mean > -0.30 -- meaning TP53 is non-essential (score above the -0.30 essentiality threshold) in the NSCLC cell line panel. Structural basis: delta-r|TP53=-0.090 (third-most disruptive node), yet TP53 is loss-of-function mutated in ~50% NSCLC including A549 (R273H) and H1299 (null). Cancer cells that have already removed TP53 do not require it for survival -- CRISPR knockout of a mutant allele has no phenotypic effect. Across the mixed 4-line panel, the mean CRISPR score should be near-zero or non-essential. This tests the structural void hypothesis: delta-r detects that TP53 is a structural coupling hub whose ABSENCE destabilizes cross-layer alignment, but this is a retrospective structural measure of cancer's past evolutionary action, not a prediction of a currently druggable target. TP53's high delta-r and near-zero CRISPR essentiality are BOTH correct -- they measure different things. If CONFIRMED (score > -0.30): TP53 is structurally important but functionally non-essential in established cancer lines -- structural void hypothesis supported. If DENIED (score < -0.30): TP53 shows residual essentiality, possibly driven by TP53-wt lines (HCC827, H460) dominating the panel mean -- would require per-line analysis to separate WT from mutant subsets.

PTEN depmap_crispr_nsclc_mean > -0.20 -- meaning PTEN is non-essential (score above -0.20) in the NSCLC cell line panel. Structural basis: delta-r|PTEN=-0.080 (fourth-most disruptive node), yet PTEN is a loss-of-function negative regulator: it dephosphorylates PIP3, suppressing AKT1 activation. In cancer cells, PTEN loss is a selective advantage -- the cell removes its own brake on PI3K signaling. CRISPR knockout of an already-non-functional or already-absent PTEN has minimal survival effect. The threshold -0.20 (less strict than TP53's -0.30) accounts for the fact that some NSCLC lines retain PTEN and knockdown might have mild effects; but genuine essentiality (< -0.50) would be unexpected. If CONFIRMED (score > -0.20): PTEN structural importance and CRISPR non-essentiality co-exist -- the structural void hypothesis holds for both tumor suppressors tested. If DENIED (score < -0.20): PTEN shows residual essentiality; requires investigating which lines drive the effect and whether it reflects PTEN's structural role or a cell-line-specific dependency.
